Burgundy
The very name of Burgundy has a sonorous
ring. Let
Paris be France’s head, Champagne her soul;

Burgundy is her stomach. It is a land of long
meals, well-supplied with the best materials
(Charolais beef to the west, Bresse chickens
to the east, and such super-creamy cheeses 
Chaource and Epoisses). It was the richest of the
ancient duchies of France. But even before France
became Christian it was famous for its wine. Burgundyis not one big vineyard,
but the name

of a province that contains several distinct and
eminent wine regions. By far the richest and most
important is the Côte d’Or, Burgundy’s heart and
ancestral home of Chardonnay and Pinot Noir,

composed of the Côte de Beaune to the south
and the Côte de Nuits to the north. In any other
context the Chardonnays
of Chablis, the reds and

whites of the Côte Chalonnaise, and the whites
of the Maconnais (all equally part of Burgundy)
would in themselves be the stars. Immediately
south of the Maconnais is Beaujolais, quite
different from Burgundy in scale, style, soil, and
grape (see pp.66-68). For all its ancient fame and riches, Burgundy
still feels simple and rustic. There is hardly 
grand house from one end of the Côte d’Or 
the other — none of the elegant country estates
that stamp the Médoc as a creation ofleisure and
wealth in the 18th and 19th centuries. Most 
the few big holdings of land, those of the Church,
were broken up by Napoleon. Burgundy is still
one of the most fragmented of France’s important
wine-growing districts. The average domaine, 
a grower’s various vineyards are called, may 
bigger than it used to be, but is still a mere 18.5

Tucked away behind the hill of Corton, some 
the east-facing vineyards to the left of the village

of Pernand-Vergelesses above once struggled
to ripen. But rising temperatures have tended 
benefit many of Burgundy’s cooler communes. Burgundy, more than in most places, itneeds to 
given the limelight. For even having pinned down
a wine to one particular climat (plot ofvines) 
one particular commune in one particular year,
it could still, in many cases, have been made
by any one of six or seven people owning small
parcels of the land, and reared in any one of six 
seven cellars. Monopoles, or whole vineyards 
the hands of one owner, are rare exceptions (see

p.53). Even the smallest grower has parcels in two
or three vineyards. score of vineyards throughout the Côte d’Or. Clos

The fragmentation of Burgundy is the cause

Côte d'Or
A Burgundian understandably feels a certain
reverence towards the rather commonplace-looking ridge of the Côte d’Or - like the
Athenians towards an unknown god. One
is bound to wonder at the fact that a few small
parcels of land on this hill give superlative wines,
each with its own positive personality, and that
others do not. Surely one can discover the factors
that distinguish one parcel from another — giving
to some grapes more sugar, thicker skins, 
generally more character and distinction. Soil and subsoil have

been analysed time and again. Temperature and
humidity and wind direction have been recorded;

wines have been minutely analysed... One can only put down
certain physical facts, and place beside them the
reputations of the great wines. No one has yet
proved conclusively how the two are connected
(even if wine-loving geologists are attracted 
the Côte d’Or like moths to a flame). The Côte lies along an important geological
fault line where the sea-bed deposits of several
different geological epochs, each rich in calcium
from defunct shellfish, are exposed like 
sliced layer cake (see opposite). Exposure has
weathered their rocks into soils of different ages
and textures; the varying degrees of slope have
mixed them in different proportions. The small
valleys, locally called combes, which lie at right
angles to the Côte add variations to the mix. The
altitude of the mid-slope is roughly constant 
about 820ft (250m), Higher, on the thinly soiled

for the highest-quality wine, but in combination
with the stones and scree washed down from the
hard limestone higher up it is perfect. Erosion
continues the blending below the actual outcrop,
the distance depending on the angle of incline. In the Côte de Beaune the marly outcrop, 
Argovien, is wider and higher on the hill; instead
of a narrow strip of vineyard under a beetling
brow of limestone there is a broad and gentle
slope vineyards can climb. The vines almost reach
the scrubby peak in places, and in these warmer
times some higher land is being (re?) converted 
‘the vine. Indeed, some villages — St-Aubin springs
most readily to mind — once thought too coo for
top-quality white wine production are coming
into their own. Burgundy was the northernmost area
in Europe to produce great red wine. It is vita that the Pinot Noir vines
ripen before the cold and damp
of autumn set in. The climate
peculiar to each vineyard, the
so-called mesoclimate (see
p.22), in combination with

Grands Crus are the first class, of which 31
are effectively in operation today, mainly 
the Côte de Nuits (see pp.58-61). The single, simple
vineyard name — Musigny, Corton, Montrachet,

or Chambertin (sometimes prefixed by “Le”) — 
the patent of Burgundy’s highest nobility. Premiers Crus, the next rank, use the name
of their commune, followed by the name of the
vineyard (or, if the wine comes from more than
one Premier Cru vineyard, the commune name
plus the words “Premier Cru”). Examples would
be, respectively, “Chambolle-Musigny, (Les)
Charmes” or, if the wine were a blend between
Charmes and another Premier Cru vineyard
or two, then the name would be “ChambolleMusigny Premier Cru”. Some Premiers Crus are
better than others, which is hardly surprising
since Burgundy has 635 in all. Perrieres 
Meursault and Rugiens in Pommard, as well 
Les Amoureuses in Chambolle-Musigny and Clos
St-Jacques in Gevrey-Chambertin, can command
prices in excess of lesser Grands Crus suchas Clos
de Vougeot and Corton. Appellation Communale is the third rank: that
is, with the right to use a commune name such 
Meursault. These wines are often referred to 
“village” wines. The name of a specific vineyard,
or lieu-dit, is permitted — and increasingly used —
on the label of these wines but it mustbe printed
in smaller type than the commune name. A few
such vineyards, while not officially Premiers Crus,
can be considered to be in the same class. Fourth, there are less propitiously sited
vineyards even within some famous communes
(typically on lower-lying land east of the main
road, the D974), which have only the right 
call their wine Bourgogne. Their produce may
be clearly inferior, but not by any means always. There are growers here who offer some of the
Côte d’Or’s rare bargains. The consumer must remember to distinguish
the name of a vineyard from that of a commune. Many villages (Vosne, Chassagne, Gevrey, etc.)
have affixed their name to that of their best
vineyard. The difference between ChevalierMontrachet (from one famous vineyard) and 
Chassagne-Montrachet (from anywhere in a big
commune) is not obvious, but itis vital. Ote d'Or vine holdings tend to be long and

The grapes of Alsace
The major clues on typical Alsace labels, most
unusually for France, are varietal. The grapes
that give their names and special qualities to the
wines of Alsace are the Riesling of the Rhine —
responsible here and in Germany for the best wine
of all: Sylvaner, Muscat, the uniquely perfumed
Gewurztraminer, and Pinots Blanc, Gris, and
Noir. Gewurztraminer is the best introduction 
the aromatic wines of Alsace: heady with scent yet
often totally clean and dry. A more precise description would
mention roses, grapefruit — sometimes lychees. It offers something much more
elusive: a balance of hard and gentle, flowery and
strong, which leads you on and never surfeits. Its
consort is Pinot Gris, the fullest-bodied but least
perfumed wine of the region; at table it offers 
realistic alternative to a white burgundy. Alsace
Muscat is usually a blend of Muscat Ottonel with
Muscat Blanc grapes. At its best it keeps all 
Muscat’s characteristic grapey scent, but makes 
dry wine as clean as a whistle: a playful apéritif. Klevener de Heiligenstein is a grape speciality

Of Portugal's many differing styles of wine, the most singular remains Vinho Verde, the youthful 'green' wine of the northernmost province, the Minho. The Minho accounts for an eighth of Portugal's wine harvest. Green is apt for this Atlantic-washed region's verdant landscape, and historically for the tart wines made from underripe grapes. This has changed dramatically as a new generation favors quality over quantity. The Minho is Portugal's wettest region. Vines are trained on trellises designed to maximize ripeness. The thick-skinned Albarinho grape dominates, resisting mildew well. Monção and Melgaço subregion produces the finest wines, sheltered from the ocean by hills making it relatively dry and warm. Lima subregion produces extremely convincing Loureiro-only wines.

The Douro Valley, home of port, has embraced a new vocation for distinctive unfortified wines labelled Douro DOC. Of all places where men have planted vineyards, the Upper Douro is the most improbable - 60-degree slopes of schist, flaking and unstable, baked in 100°F summer sun. The vine is one of few plants not deterred by these conditions. The harsh climate, ranging from Atlantic-influenced in the west to increasingly continental inland, suits it. Engineering feats of building walls along mountainsides created patches for planting. More than 6,280 acres were replanted with approved varieties rather than the traditional jumble. The Douro claims to be the world's first demarcated wine region (1756). The Cima Corgo remains the heartland of port production. Each of the 140,000 vineyard plots are rated A to F, determining which may make port each year.

The grapes for port may be grown in the wilderness of the Douro Valley, but about two-thirds of the wine is still aged in the huddles of shippers' lodges in Vila Nova de Gaia across the river from Oporto. Port is made by running off partially fermented red wine into a tank or barrel a quarter full of spirits, stopping fermentation so the resulting mixture is both strong and sweet. Vintage port is bottled at two years and named with shipper's name and year, made only in good vintages. Most port goes through blending to emerge as branded wine. Aged tawnies, labelled 10 or 20 years, can cost as much as vintage ports. Colheita ports are wood-aged ports from a single year. White port is made from white grapes. Late Bottled Vintage (LBV) is port kept in barrel for four to six years.

Bairrada and Dão are two long-established names in the throes of beneficial transition. Bairrada lies between the granite hills of Dão and the Atlantic coast, its nearness to the Atlantic making wines naturally fresh. The best wines come from clay-limestone that gives body and typical Portuguese bite to its 85% red wine. The defining ingredient is the indigenous Baga grape, likened to Nebbiolo for its heavy charge of acids and tannins, producing wines of formidable character demanding 20 years' cellaring. Dão is effectively a granite plateau where bare rocks show through well-drained soil. The Serra do Caramulo shields it from the Atlantic and Serra da Estrela protects the southeast. Touriga Nacional, possibly at its best in Dão, shows great promise for long ageing. Encruzado has proved one of Portugal's finest single-varietal white wine grapes.

Vinho Regional Lisboa, once called Estremadura, is one of Portugal's most productive wine regions. Its long Atlantic-coastal growing season, choice of soils, and new interest in better grapes are giving ambitious estates their chance. Historic wines Colares (tannic red on the west coast), Carcavelos (now mainly a surfing beach), and Bucelas (fresh white from lemony Arinto) are threatened with extinction. The Setúbal Peninsula between Tagus and Sado estuaries has clay-limestone hills around Azeitão cooled by Atlantic winds, and hotter inland sandy plain east of Palmela. The region's traditional wine is Moscatel de Setúbal, a rich, pale-orange Muscat lightly fortified and highly perfumed by long maceration with aromatic Muscat of Alexandria skins.

In the vast and diverse Alentejo region, vineyards are concentrated in four main DOC enclaves: Borba, Redondo, Reguengos, and Vidigueira. The Alentejo's wide, sun-browned spaces are dotted with silver olive trees and dark cork oaks. Ranch-like estates are the norm. Even in midwinter, this is a land of sun and open vistas - Spain is just over the border. Rainfall is low and temperatures routinely high with picking starting in the third week of August. Rich, loamy soils are interspersed with granite and especially schist. Six of the eight DOC subregions are based on an important co-op, none more important than Reguengos. Aragonés (Tempranillo) and local Trincadeira have risen to the fore, along with red-fleshed Alicante Bouschet which takes on unaccustomed nobility here. Portalegre to the north is cooler and wetter with granite and schist.

The ancients knew these offshore volcanoes as the Enchanted Isles, 400 miles off the coast of Morocco. Madeira is one of the prettiest islands in the world, as steep as an iceberg and as green as a glade. Wine has been the principal product for 400 years. The settlers planted the island with Malvasia grape, concentrated its sugar in the sun. The sweet-and-sour result was adequate as ballast on sailing ships and anti-scurvy protection. Crossing the equator mellowed madeira wonderfully. Today, madeira is subjected to gentle ordeal by fire - warming the wine to almost 122°F in hot stores (estufas) for at least three months. Traditional grape varieties: Malmsey (Malvasia) is sweetest and earliest maturing; Bual is lighter and slightly less sweet; Verdelho is made less sweet and softer; Sercial makes the driest, most revitalizing wine from the island's highest vineyards. Tinta Negra accounts for about 86% of all grapes.

Galicia's best-known wines are as far from the Spanish stereotype as possible to imagine: delicate, lively, aromatic whites that go perfectly with the shellfish that is the standard Galician diet. Everything about Rías Baixas wine is small scale except Martin Códax. This damp, green corner of Spain was until recently extremely poor and virtually ignored. The landscape is exceptional: irregular Atlantic inlets called rías, effectively shallow fjords, lined with densely forested hills. Vines have traditionally been trained on pergolas, horizontal trellises above shoulder height, allowing other crops beneath. The thick-skinned Albarino grape dominates, resisting mildew that persistently threatens. Val do Salnés is the most important subzone, and the coolest and dampest. O Rosal to the south produces wines notably lower in acidity. Condado do Tea is the warmest subzone, furthest from the coast.

In recent years the climate has grown steadily warmer in eastern and southern Spain. At the same time the pendulum of fashion has swung back in favour of wines that refresh rather than merely impress - a cue for cool, damp northwest Spain. Traditions here are Celtic. The Atlantic, hills, wind, and rain are the chief physical factors. Wines are mainly light, dry, and refreshing. Ribeiro shipped wine to England in the middle ages, long before the Douro Valley. Ribeira Sacra makes Galicia's potentially most interesting red wine on almost impossibly steep slate terraces above the rivers Sil and Miño. Valdeorras has established a name for firm white Godello. Mencía is the basis of fashionable Bierzo, one of Spain's fruitiest, most aromatic, distinctively refreshing reds, grown just over the border in Castilla y León but in conditions very like Galicia.

Ribera del Duero is the modern red wine miracle of northern Spain. Barely known in the early 1980s, it now rivals Rioja as Spain's foremost red wine region. The plain of Old Castile is traversed by the adolescent Duero, which in Portugal becomes the Douro. At 3,000ft (850m) the nights are remarkably cool: in late August it can be 95°F at noon and 54°F at night. Spring frosts are common. The light and air have high-altitude dryness and brightness, as do the wines with particularly lively acidity. Vega Sicilia provided initial proof that very fine red wine could be made here, planted in the 1860s. Its Unico, aged longer in oak than virtually any other table wine, is a wine of astonishing, penetrating personality. Here Bordeaux grapes add glamour to native Tempranillo (called Tinto Fino or Tinta del País). There were just 24 bodegas in 1982; by 2012 there were more than 200.

In 1998, medieval town of Toro had eight bodegas and its wine was widely regarded as rustic. But the sheer exuberance of its local strain of Tempranillo, Tinta de Toro, became too obvious to ignore. By 2012 there were 50 bodegas. High-profile investors included owners of Vega Sicilia, Pesquera, and Mauro. The key to Toro's quality is altitude: at 1,970-2,460ft above sea level, growers can depend on cool nights to fix colour and flavour. Average vine age is relatively high. Most are still grown as individual bush-vines, spaced as far as 10ft apart - a record low vine density imposed by near-desert conditions with under 16in of rain a year. Neighbouring Rueda is historically a producer of white wine from local Verdejo, rescued when Rioja's Marqués de Riscal moved white wine production here in the 1970s. Verdejo yields wines as refreshing as Sauvignon Blanc.

Camped round the northeastern boundary of Rioja, Navarra was long in competition with it until Bordeaux merchants chose to place their post-phylloxera trade with Rioja and its ready rail link from Haro. For most of the 20th century Navarra's scattered vineyards were dedicated chiefly to Garnacha and useful rosados. Then came a revolution with Cabernet, Merlot, Tempranillo, and Chardonnay. Navarra is no more homogeneous than Rioja with a world of difference between the hot, dry, flat Ribera Baja and Ribera Alta subzones in the south and the cooler northern subzones. The best Garnachas in Ribera Baja come from Fitero because its poor, Châteauneuf-like soils are open to Mediterranean warmth. Tierra Estella and Valdizarbe in the north have varied aspects and elevations requiring careful site selection. Arinzano earned Pago status, Spain's highest single-estate denomination.

Rioja established itself 150 years ago as Spain's nearest shot at French-style wine. It is still Spain's most familiar name abroad. Without the massive wall of the rocky Sierra de Cantabria, Rioja would be too buffeted by Atlantic winds for vines to survive. In the east, vines ripen fully at 2,600ft thanks to Mediterranean warming. The region is divided into three zones: Rioja Alta (western, higher part south of the River Ebro), Rioja Alavesa (Basque, north bank of river), and Rioja Baja (extensive hotter eastern section). Tempranillo accounts for 61% of vineyards, blending well with plumper Garnacha (18%). The region's reputation was made in the late 19th century when Bordeaux négociants came here after phylloxera. With rail links to the Atlantic coast, Haro was the ideal centre. Spanish bodegas traditionally aged wine longer than is customary, releasing wine when ready to drink rather than ready to sell.

Catalunya has convinced the world that it is a country apart from Spain. Barcelona is one of Europe's dynamic cities, as close to France as to Castile. Catalans are busy and on the wine front, exceptionally creative. Most obviously there is Cava, Spain's answer to champagne, of which 95% is produced in Catalunya mainly from vineyards in and around Sant Sadurní d'Anoia in Penedès. The industry is dominated by Codorníu and Freixenet. Macabeo dominates most Cava blends, with distinctive local flavour from Xarel-lo and crispness from Parellada. For still wines, Penedès is the leading DO. International vine varieties are more widespread here than anywhere else in Spain, beginning in the 1960s with pioneers Jean Leon and Miguel Torres. The Tarragona DO also supplies material for Cava. Higher western vineyards have their own DO of Montsant, encircling the Priorat DOCa.

Priorat is not a name you will find in old reference books. Before phylloxera there were 12,350 acres of vineyards in this dizzying landscape of crinkle-folded hills. By 1979, when René Barbier first saw the potential, there were only 1,500 acres of mainly Cariñena left. In 1989 he persuaded five friends to share winemaking premises in Gratallops. Their wines were quite distinct from rustic, raisiny ferments - concentrated, mineral-laden wines very different from the oaky Spanish norm. Priorat is protected from the northwest by the Sierra de Montsant. Its particularly unusual soil, llicorella, a dark-brown slate whose jagged rock faces sparkle with quartzite, makes the best Priorat the almost chewable essence it is. Annual rainfall is often less than 16in, but Priorat's soils are unusually cool and damp so vine roots tunnel Douro-style through faults. The Vi de Vila scheme introduced in 2009 established 12 village wine zones.
content.soil: llicorella

content.overview: For perhaps 20 centuries, wine in Andalucía meant vinos generosos: sherry above all, but also Montilla-Moriles and Málaga. Sherry is arguably still Spain's greatest wine. The key to producing wines with freshness as well as southern ripeness is altitude. The sherry country between Cádiz and Seville is almost a caricature of grandee Spain. Sherry's great distinction is finesse - a question of chalk, of the Palomino Fino grape, of huge investment and long-inherited skill. The chalk areas (albarizas) are best; the pagos of Carrascal, Macharnudo, Añina, and Balbaina the most famous. Fino matures under a protective layer of flor yeast. Olorosos are matured in contact with air, fortified to prevent flor growth. Amontillado is an old fino that put on weight with age. Manzanilla from Sanlúcar de Barrameda is blessed with a faintly salty tang. Montilla-Moriles uses Pedro Ximénez grape and doesn't require fortification due to naturally strong musts.
content.soil: albariza

italy.name: Italy
italy.overview:
Is there a country so stylishly creative as Italy? Or less easily governed? Italy has the world's richest variety of individual wine styles, distinctive terroirs, and indigenous grape varieties. At the top end her wines have a vivacity, originality, savour, and flair all their own. Colonizing Greeks called Italy Oenotria — 'the land of wine' (or, strictly, staked vines — a sure sign of viticultural ambition). Only France — sometimes — makes more wine than Italy. In terms of geography, Italy cannot fail to produce good wine in great variety, if slopes, sunshine, and a temperate climate are the essentials. Her peculiar physique, that of a long spine of mountains reaching south from the sheltering Alps almost to North Africa, means that there can hardly be a desirable combination of altitude with latitude and exposure that is absent. Many of her soils are volcanic; much is limestone; there is plenty of gravelly clay.


Wine Law: From the 1960s on, the Italian government undertook the monumental task of devising an answer to France's Appellation d'Origine Contrôlée system: the DOC (Denominazione di Origine Controllata), complete with boundaries, maximum yields, and specified grape varieties and production methods. A superior form of DOC, DOCG (for which the origin was not just controlled, but guaranteed) was created and has been increasingly bestowed since the 1980s. In 1992, a law was passed to restructure the whole system with tighter restrictions. Like France's Vins de Pays, IGTs can use the geographical and varietal name and, crucially, vintage.

content.overview: Nebbiolo finds its most dazzling expression in the Langhe hills, on the calcareous clay soils of the right bank of the River Tanaro, to the northeast of Alba in the Barbaresco zone. Barbaresco is always a tannic wine that needs to age, one in which the tannin merely frames a stunning array of haunting flavours. Great Barbarescos can overlay smoky sweetness, the flavour of raspberries, leather and spice, leafy lightness on jam-like concentration. The village of just 600 people lies on a ridge that wobbles west towards Alba, flanked all the way by vineyards of renown. Asili, Martinenga, and Sori Tildin are bywords for the finest reds. The authorities have divided the entire Barbaresco zone into contiguous subzones, some of very much better quality than others.

Barbaresco is a great example of Nebbiolo, but Barolo is the greatest. The Barolo zone starts just two miles southwest of Barbaresco, with the Dolcetto vineyards of Diano d'Alba lying between. There are now 4,460 acres (1,886ha) of Barolo vineyards concentrated in 11 communes in the relatively populous Langhe hills. To the west of the Alba road around La Morra, soils are calcareous marls from the Tortonian epoch. These western hills tend to offer slightly less tense, more openly fragrant wines. The great vineyards here include Brunate, Cerequio, Le Rocche, and La Serra in La Morra, and Cannubi in Barolo. To the east, in Castiglione Falletto, Serralunga d'Alba, and north of Monforte d'Alba, soils are Helvetian, much less fertile, with more sandstone. They tend to produce even more concentrated wines that demand extremely long ageing.

The Adige Valley forms the dramatic corridor into the Alps that links Italy with Austria over the Brenner Pass. It is a rock-walled trench, widening in places to give views of distant peaks. The catch-all DOC for the whole valley is Trentino. From the northern end of Trentino comes purple Teroldego, grown on the cliff-hemmed, pergola-carpeted, gravelly plain known as the Campo Rotaliano. Teroldego Rotaliano is one of Italy's great characters, with hallmark bracing acidity and the hint of bitterness that marks it as a native.

The Alto Adige, the southern tip of Austria's Tyrol, is Italy's most northerly wine region and one of its most vigorous, its serious aspirations to quality increasingly fulfilled. Its Alpine peaks proclaim both a cultural and a viticultural melting pot. German is a more common language than Italian, yet French grape varieties have been more widespread than Teutonic ones. Production is centred on the benchland and lower slopes of the Adige Valley. Vineyard altitudes vary from 650 to almost 3,300ft (200-1,000m). The workhorse red grape is the Schiava (alias Vernatsch). The local Lagrein, originally grown around Bolzano, produces much more serious stuff.

The hills of Verona, stretching from Soave westwards to Lake Garda, have such fertile volcanic soil that vegetation grows uncontrollably. The Veneto has become Italy's most productive wine region. Real Soave is incomparable, with its insistent combination of almonds and lemons. The important grapes are Garganega and Verdicchio (called Trebbiano di Soave). Soave cohabits with Valpolicella. Plain Valpolicella should have a lovely cherry colour and flavour, lively acidity, a gentle sweet smell, and just a trace of almond bitterness. The most potent form of Valpolicella is a Recioto or Amarone, respectively the sweet and dry results of drying selected, healthy grapes off the vine.

The original, northernmost quarter of what might be called Tuscany's Gold Coast, the Maremma Toscana, a stretch of land from Bibbona as far south as the Argentario Peninsula that has attracted a fever of interest and outside investment. This once-malarial coast has no long wine tradition; the flame was lit in the 1940s when the Marchese Incisa della Rocchetta chose a stony hectare of his wife's extensive San Guido estate in Bolgheri to plant Cabernet. His nephews, Piero and Lodovico Antinori, tasted the wines and Antinori started to bottle and market Sassicaia with the 1968 vintage. By the mid-1970s it was world-famous.

The hills between Florence and Siena can come as near to the Roman poet's idea of gentlemanly country life as anywhere on earth. The original Chianti zone, the first anywhere in the world to be delimited, in 1716, was limited to the land around the villages of Radda, Gaiole, and Castellina, with Greve added later. This today produces Chianti Classico. Chianti Classico is now an extremely serious wine made substantially (80-100% of the blend) from low-cropped, top-quality Sangiovese vines, aged in wood with a life expectancy of 10 years or even more. In 1975, the ancient Antinori family launched their rebel flag Tignanello, made from Sangiovese with a small proportion of Cabernet, giving rise to the Super Tuscan phenomenon.

In the 1970s, Montalcino was the poorest hilltop town in southern Tuscany. The climate here is more equable than farther north or south. Monte Amiata, rising to 5,600ft (1,700m) just to the south, collects the summer storms. Montalcino has the warm, dry climate of the Tuscan coast with, in the best vineyards, the rockier, less fertile soils of the cooler Chianti Classico zone. Clemente Santi and his kin (now called Biondi-Santi) were establishing a model for what they labelled Brunello di Montalcino. The zone has been expanded enormously, from just over 150 acres (60ha) in 1960 to more than 5,000 acres (2,000ha) today. Altitudes vary from 490ft (150m) in the Val d'Orcia in the south to 1,640ft (500m) just south of Montalcino itself.

Montalcino's neighbours to the east have ancient pretensions of their own embodied in their aristocratic DOCG, Vino Nobile di Montepulciano. Montepulciano is a hill town of great charm surrounded by vineyards planted with a mixture of Sangiovese, called here Prugnolo Gentile, and some local and Bordeaux varieties. Vino Nobile must contain a minimum of 70% Sangiovese. Another of Montepulciano's triumphs is its Vin Santo, the forgotten luxury of many parts of Italy, made from grapes carefully dried in well-ventilated premises.

germany.name: Germany
germany.overview:
After a late-20th-century wobble German wine has steadily been working its way back into international favour as new wine drinkers discover its unique qualities, its freshness, vibrancy, and perfume. Sheer mass in wine eventually loses its appeal; German wines offer positive refreshment. And a new generation of growers has taken up the reins. Their interpretation of their country's great traditions is turning the tide in Germany's favour again. Many of Germany's best vineyards lie almost as far north as grapes can be persuaded to ripen. Some of the best are on land unfit for normal agriculture; if there were no vines there would be forest and bare mountain. Their secret is the balance of sugar and acidity. But in good years the two are so finely counterpoised that they have the inevitability of great art. Thanks to all that acidity, the best also age magnificently - far better than most other white wines.


Wine Law: The classic gems in Germany's crown are sweet wines. But sweetness is unfashionable and this has been seen as a commercial handicap. The result? Far drier German wines, with 64% classified trocken (dry) or halbtrocken (medium dry). Quality designations include: Pradikatswein (wines from naturally ripest grapes with no chaptalization) with levels Kabinett, Spatlese, Auslese, Beerenauslese (BA), Eiswein, and Trockenbeerenauslese (TBA). Grosses Gewachs is the VDP designation for top-quality dry wines from specified top sites.

content.overview: The spectacular river walls of slate mapped here, rising over 700ft (200m) in places, were first planted with vines by the Romans in the 4th century. They provide perfect conditions for Riesling, introduced here in the 15th century. The wines of the river vary along its banks even more than the wines of Burgundy vary along the Cote d'Or. But all the best sites face south, held up to the sun like toast to a fire. Piesporter Goldtropfchen's dramatic amphitheatre gives Piesport a standing far above its neighbours. Its honeyed wines have magical fragrance and breeding, which can, thanks to the particularly deep, clay-like slate here, exude almost baroque aromas.

The view from the ruined castle above Bernkastel is of a green wall of vines 700ft (200m) high and 5 miles (8km) long. Only the Douro, in Portugal, in the whole gazetteer of rivers to which the vine is wedded, has anything approaching a comparable sight. The Mosel's most famous vineyard starts abruptly, rising almost sheer above the gables of the tourist mecca that is Bernkastel: dark slate frowning at slate. The butt of the hill, its one straight south elevation, is the Doctor vineyard. The trademark of Bernkastel is a touch of flint. Wehleners, grown on shallow stony slate, are rich and filigreed while those grown on the deeper, heavier slate of Graach are somehow earthier.

united-states.name: United States
united-states.overview:
The USA is the world's most important consumer of wine, and only France, Spain, and Italy make more of it. Canada has become a serious producer in recent years, and even Mexico is starting to show signs of catching up. Not just the long-established West Coast but scattered spots all over the continent hold at least local interest for wine drinkers. America is entering a new era as the second wine continent.

When the early colonists first landed in North America, they were impressed by the rampant grapevines whose fruit festooned the forests. The grapes were sweet, if strange, to taste. It was natural to assume that wine would be one of the good things of the New World. Yet more than 300 years of American history were a saga of the shattered hopes of would-be wine-growers. European vinifera vines planted in the new colonies withered and died.

The American soil was riddled with the European vine's deadliest enemy, phylloxera. The hot, humid summers of the south and east encouraged diseases unknown in Europe. In the north, European vines died in the harsh winters. Native American vines had developed resistance to all of these hazards.

We now know of well over a dozen vine species indigenous to North America, many of them (and particularly Vitis labrusca) producing wine so feral that it has long been described as 'foxy'. Once American and European vines coexisted on this continent, their genes commingled in random and spontaneous combinations from which various varieties with less obvious foxiness emerged. The Alexander grape, discovered in Pennsylvania, was the earliest of these accidental American-European hybrids; Catawba, Delaware, and Isabella followed. Norton is an all-American grape still producing powerfully distinctive and not-at-all foxy reds.

By the mid-19th century, America had two wine industries, poles apart. California, where vinifera was well established by the late 19th century, enjoyed an early golden age in the 1880s and 1890s, only to see its burgeoning wine industry besieged by the scourges of mildew and phylloxera.

But then came a blow greater than any of these: the prohibition of alcohol throughout North America between 1919 and 1933. The lasting legacy of the culture that spawned this outright ban on all things alcoholic has been a wine industry long thwarted by unnecessarily complex organization and obstructive legislation. Despite this, wine is currently basking in the glow of fashion and enthusiasm bordering on obsession among a growing proportion of Americans.

content.overview:
Ninety per cent of all American wine is grown in California: more wine than in any country outside Europe. And the planting goes on. Here at the vine's western limit there is a distinct shortage of geographical generalities and physical truisms. California's wine geography presents a series of surprises, and much more variety than outsiders give it credit for. The potential of a vineyard site is linked hardly at all to latitude but is crucially determined by what lies between it and the Pacific Ocean. The more mountains there are between the site and the sea, the less chance there is of marine air, often fog, reaching it to moderate the climate.

So cold is the inshore water of the Pacific here that it causes a perpetual fog bank all summer just off the coast. Each day that the temperatures approach 90°F (32°C) inland, the rising hot air draws the fog inland to fill its space. The Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco straddles its most famous pathway, but everywhere up and down the coast where the Coast Ranges dip below about 1,500ft (460m), cold Pacific air spills over and cools the land.

The Central (or San Joaquin) Valley on the other hand, the flat farmland that still makes agriculture California's most important economic activity (and grows three-quarters of the state's wine grapes), is too far inland to be directly influenced by the Pacific. It is one of the world's sunniest wine regions, with a hotter, drier climate than anywhere else.

Fashion has always been important in California. Wine producers and consumers tend to act more uniformly than you would normally expect in a geographical unit roughly half as big as France. Recent vineyard trends have included better matching of grape variety to a specific site, more densely planted vines from a wider variety of clones, with much more controlled, less-dense foliage, more precise irrigation, and a new appreciation of freshness as opposed to super-ripeness.
content.climate: Mediterranean with strong Pacific influence. Summers are very dry with rainfall concentrated in first few months of year. Fog is crucial cooling mechanism. Climate varies dramatically from cool coastal to hot inland.
content.soil: Half of the soil orders on earth are found in California. Volcanic, alluvial, clay, loam, and ancient marine sediments all present.
key_facts: Marine fog is critical to moderating temperatures

content.overview:
Twenty per cent of the value of all California's wine comes from the Napa Valley — from only 4% of its volume. Such is the reputation of the world's most glamorous, most cosseted, and most heavily capitalized wine region. Its modern history starts in 1966, with the construction of the Robert Mondavi winery. The mission-style adobe arch and global ambitions of this iconic cellar signalled the start of the transformation of a sleepy farming community of walnut and prune orchards into a monoculture of 46,000 acres (18,620ha) of vines that is far more varied than most outsiders realize.

Half of the soil orders on earth are to be found in the Napa Valley. In broad terms the valley is the result of the Napa River eroding its way between the Mayacamas Mountains on the west and the Vaca range on the east. Their respective peaks are the igneous outflows of Mount Veeder in the west and Atlas Peak and Mount George in the east.

As for climate, the open (southern) end of this narrow valley is much cooler than the northern end, by an average of at least 10°F (6.3°C) during the summer. Wines tend to taste progressively richer with riper tannins as you move north. As you move up into the hills, they have more structure and concentration than the valley floor counterparts.

Cabernet Sauvignon is the Napa Valley's grape. Napa's best Cabernets are incontrovertibly some of the world's most successful. They have unparalleled opulence and exuberance, yet the finest examples have rigour, too.
content.climate: Average growing season temperature 66.8°F (19.3°C) at St Helena. Annual rainfall 36.6in (931mm). Cool southern end warmed by San Pablo Bay influence; hot northern end around Calistoga. Marine air drawn through gaps in western hills.
content.soil: Extremely varied - thinnest, oldest, and least fertile on valley sides; deep fertile alluvial clays on valley floor; well-drained benchlands on both sides. Volcanic influence significant.
key_facts: Produces 20% of California wine value from 4% of volume
Principal viticultural hazards: over-winter drought, spring frost, heat spikes, autumn rain
Principal grapes: Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Chardonnay, Zinfandel

content.overview:
To explain Rutherford to a visitor schooled in French wine, you might describe it as the Pauillac of California. This is Cabernet country par excellence. Nearly two-thirds of the 3,500 acres (1,417ha) of vines here are Cabernet Sauvignon and most of the rest are other Bordeaux red grapes grown to complement it. Not that the Cabernet ripened in Rutherford's warm summers is left with any of the holes on the palate that in the Médoc may require Merlot and Cabernet Franc to fill them. Rutherford's is full-throttle Cabernet in terms of ripeness, even if it typically tends to have more structure, backbone, and longevity than almost any other California Cabernet.

Rutherford has produced great age-worthy Cabernet since the first half of the 20th century, when names such as Inglenook and Beaulieu carved California's reputation. The proof is still there, behind labels printed in the 1940s.

All of these are on the so-called Rutherford Bench on the west side of the valley, a slightly elevated stretch of sedimentary gravelly sand and alluvial fans carved out by the influential Napa River. Soils are deep and particularly well-drained, so conducive to lower crop loads, earlier ripening, and greater intensity of flavour. Many tasters detect a mineral element in wines produced here, known in shorthand as 'Rutherford dust'.
content.climate: Warm summers, some cooling marine influence from San Pablo Bay reaches this far north. Relatively homogeneous elevation up to 600ft (180m).
content.soil: Rutherford Bench: sedimentary gravelly sand and alluvial fans. Deep, well-drained soils conducive to lower crop loads and greater intensity.
key_facts: Nearly two-thirds planted to Cabernet Sauvignon
Home to historic Inglenook and Beaulieu vineyards
Rutherford Bench on west side is prime vineyard land

content.overview:
There may not be much more to Oakville than the Oakville Grocery store and the Napa Wine Company (a popular custom crush facility). But it was in Oakville, in 1966, that the California wine revolution began when Robert Mondavi built his striking winery. Going north up the valley, Oakville is where really serious, meaty Cabernet begins. The wines may not have quite the weight and backbone of the best that Rutherford can offer but they certainly offer the Napa Valley's quintessential opulence. Oakville can also produce some vote-catching Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc, even some convincing Sangiovese, but Cabernet is its essence.

The To Kalon vineyard in the west was planted as long ago as 1868. Such is the sheer, exuberant quality of its Cabernet that its precise boundaries, ownership, and name have been fought over but are now shared by Robert Mondavi and Beckstoffer Vineyards. The first Napa Valley vineyard to achieve international fame in the modern era was Martha's Vineyard, bottled and label-designated by Joe Heitz.

Today the name of Oakville is so potent that several of its producers allocate their wines rather than sell them, and donate a small proportion to America's charity auctions and marvel at the zeroes. Screaming Eagle in the east and the increasing range of wines from the Harlan estate on the opposite side of the valley are the most obvious examples.
content.climate: Far enough south to feel some marine influence from San Pablo Bay. AVA boundary goes up to 600ft (180m) contour line.
content.soil: West side: alluvial fans with large particles washed from Mayacamas Mountains providing good drainage. Valley floor: richer Bale loams. East side: heavier soils with more volcanic influence.
key_facts: Site of Robert Mondavi winery (1966) - start of California wine revolution
Screaming Eagle and Harlan Estate among most sought-after wines
Martha's Vineyard first Napa vineyard to achieve international fame

content.overview:
Stags Leap is the eastern opposite number to Yountville; the AVA for an enclave contained behind a high knoll on the valley floor and climbing the eastern hills of the Vaca range. Fame came overnight in 1976, when a Cabernet from Stag's Leap Wine Cellars came first in a Paris tasting that still makes the news four decades later. It pitted some of what were then California's better-known wines against some of the best of Bordeaux. Much to everyone's surprise, the California wines repeated the performance in a rerun exactly 30 years later.

Of all Napa Cabernets, those of Stags Leap have arguably the most recognizable character: a silky texture, a certain aroma of violets or cherries, tannins that have always been supple, power with more delicacy than is usual in Napa Cabernet. Some make comparisons with St-Julien, others with Margaux.

The district, barely three miles by one, takes its name from a run of bare rocks, a basalt palisade, on the eastern edge of the valley, an afternoon suntrap from which warm air radiates. The heat is moderated by marine breezes, another afternoon phenomenon, which are funnelled through the Golden Gate, over San Pablo Bay.
content.climate: Warm enough for vines to start leafing two weeks ahead of more northerly areas, though ripening is slower. Marine breezes moderate afternoon heat.
content.soil: Moderately fertile volcanic gravel-loams on valley floor; rockier, well-drained terrain on hillsides.
key_facts: Barely three miles by one in size
Cabernets known for silky texture and violet/cherry aromas
Named for basalt palisade on eastern edge
Shafer Hillside Select among most celebrated wines

content.overview:
In less than 20 years the 100-plus miles of the Central Coast have spawned a clutch of California's most fashionable wine regions. It stretches all the way from the vineyards south of San Francisco Bay to the subtropical climate of greater Los Angeles. The two counties in between, San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara, were transformed by the end of the 20th century from scrub oaks and cattle grazing to an undulating strip of vineyards.

This part of California is geologically distinctive: the San Andreas Fault runs down the eastern side of the region; its vineyards are based on quite different, more marine-influenced, carbonate-infused soils, which is one of the reasons Central Coast wines have a certain voluptuousness. The climate here is primarily maritime, with mild winters and summers much cooler than the California norm.

San Luis Obispo County has at least three distinct zones, two of them in the vast Paso Robles AVA. The historic fame of Paso Robles comes largely from potent Zinfandels. This was the area chosen by the Perrin family of Chateau de Beaucastel to plant French clones of Rhone grapes at its Tablas Creek nursery and winery.

Santa Maria Valley is southern California's riposte to the Cote d'Or, providing conditions that are, if anything, cooler still. Its river runs out to sea in flat land that offers no opposition at all to the Pacific air. The best grapes, Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, are grown on slopes high enough above the valley floor to be on the fringe of the fog belt. Their naturally high acidity is offset by a fruity intensity.
content.climate: Average growing season temperature 60.7°F (16.0°C) at Santa Maria. Annual rainfall 14in (354mm). Primarily maritime with mild winters. Long growing season builds flavour over months. Principal hazard: late ripening.
content.soil: Marine-influenced, carbonate-infused soils. Varied - from sandy to calcareous depending on location.
key_facts: Stretches from San Francisco Bay to Los Angeles
Paso Robles famous for Zinfandel and Rhone varieties
Tablas Creek introduced many Rhone clones
Santa Maria Valley rivals Burgundy for Pinot Noir
Sta. Rita Hills AVA excellent for Pinot Noir

content.overview:
Oregon and Washington, the two major players in the Pacific Northwest, could hardly be more different. Damp, green Oregon has always been the home of the craftsman winemaker, vinifying grapes grown in small, personally managed, mainly estate vineyards, many of which are organic. The Coast Range lines up as a sheltering sea wall, but here the ocean's warm North Pacific current brings rain instead of fog, modifying what might otherwise be more severe temperatures and resulting in some of America's most nearly Burgundian wines.

Arid, continental eastern Washington, on the other hand, originally operated on a quasi-industrial scale, with heavily irrigated grapes picked mechanically and shipped back to the wineries that clustered round Seattle. Today, Washington is the second most important wine-growing state in the US, with 40,000 acres of vines producing some of America's most admired Cabernet, Merlot, Riesling, and, especially, Syrah.

Oregon, meanwhile, has become a little less homespun in its organization. The total area devoted to the vine grew by 50% to 20,500 acres in the six years to 2011.
content.climate: Oregon: maritime with cool, wet conditions. Washington: continental semi-desert east of Cascades with irrigation essential. Significant temperature variation between Oregon and eastern Washington.
content.soil: Oregon: Jory volcanic loams in Willamette. Washington: varied, often sandy and fast-draining, including Missoula flood deposits.
key_facts: Washington is USA's second-largest wine-growing state
Oregon famous for Pinot Noir rivaling Burgundy
Washington excels with Cabernet, Merlot, Riesling, Syrah

content.overview:
Climate is Oregon's great point of difference: difference from both California to the south and Washington State to the north. While Willamette Valley summers are cooler and cloudier than those of sunny California, its winters are considerably milder than those of Washington's deeply continental wine country in the east of the state. Pacific Ocean clouds and humidity wash across Oregon's wineland, especially the northern reaches of the Willamette Valley, through breaks in the Coastal Range.

The discovery of the Willamette Valley as a modern wine region was made in the late 1960s at Dundee in Yamhill County by David Lett, when he established his Eyrie Vineyards. Had Lett planted Chardonnay and Cabernet, fame would have been slow to follow. But he hit on Pinot Noir. Since the mid-1970s Oregon and Pinot Noir have been inextricably linked.

Almost as though this were the way Pinot Noir liked to be grown, the Willamette Valley has largely remained small-scale. High-profile foreigners moved in: from Burgundy itself, Robert Drouhin and his daughter Veronique at Domaine Drouhin.

After considerable debate, several sub-appellations are now officially recognized within the 150-mile-long Willamette Valley. Dundee Hills has established itself as the epicentre for top-quality wine, thanks to its famous heavy, red-tinged Jory loam soils.
content.climate: Average growing season temperature 60.6°F (15.9°C) at McMinnville. Annual rainfall 41.7in (1,060mm). Principal hazards: fungal diseases, underripeness. Vintage pattern as wilful as any in France.
content.soil: Dundee Hills: heavy red-tinged Jory loam. Green Valley and Sebastopol Hills: sandy Goldridge soil. Yamhill-Carlton: varied with some frost problems.
key_facts: Eyrie Vineyards established 1960s - Oregon's Pinot pioneer
Six sub-appellations: Dundee Hills, Yamhill-Carlton, McMinnville, Eola-Amity Hills, Chehalem Mountains, Ribbon Ridge
International Pinot Noir Celebration held annually in McMinnville

content.overview:
Between the Appalachians and Chesapeake Bay, its white-fenced grasslands in the lee of the Blue Ridge Mountains devoted to blue-blooded horses, Virginia seems a world away from the politics of Washington DC. Yet the northernmost vineyards are well under an hour's drive from Capitol Hill.

Wine has been playing a more important part in the official vision of this US state than in any other. It had an unpromising start, as Thomas Jefferson found to his cost at Monticello, the spiritual home of the Virginia wine industry. What no-one knew — that European vines need American rootstocks to protect them from phylloxera — is now well known.

Even today, vinifera vines in Virginia have to battle the climate: a relatively short growing season and a hot and humid summer, with few cool nights before September. Winters can be so cold that soils take their time to warm up, and buds are rarely seen before late April. In 1980, there were only five wineries; by 2012, there were closer to 200 and more than 80% of grapes were vinifera varieties.

Cabernet Franc has shown the most affinity for the vineyards of northern and central Virginia. Virginia's growers decided some years ago that Viognier would be their signature grape. Petit Verdot and Petit Manseng are more recent Virginia specialities.
content.climate: Average growing season temperature 66.1°F (18.9°C) at Charlottesville. Annual rainfall 42.7in (1,085mm). Principal hazard: high summer rainfall. Continental with hot, humid summers and cold winters.
content.soil: Many soils have high clay content. Ambitious growers seek slopes that drain quickly after summer downpours.
key_facts: Thomas Jefferson's Monticello is spiritual home of Virginia wine
Viognier adopted as signature white grape
Cabernet Franc shows most affinity for local conditions

content.overview:
New Yorkers tend to pride themselves on drinking the wines of the world, but their own state makes more wine than any other in the USA — other than California. Admittedly 20,000 of its 34,000 acres of vineyard are planted with American vines, typically Vitis labrusca producing grapes for grape juice and jelly.

Like Ontario across the border, the rest of New York State is busy reinventing itself as a serious wine producer. The majority of New York wineries are less than 20 years old, sprouting most noticeably in the Finger Lakes (about 100 wineries), Long Island (more than 50), and the Hudson River Region (more than 30).

Long Island, air-conditioned and occasionally battered by the Atlantic, is New York's youngest wine region and the state's answer to Bordeaux. The Finger Lakes are slowly carving out a reputation for particularly fine, almost Saar-like dry, age-worthy Rieslings. Riesling, with its hard wood, can withstand low temperatures well and has proved a much better fit here than Chardonnay.
content.climate: Long Island: maritime with long growing season. Finger Lakes: extreme continental with fewer than 200 frost-free days in many parts. Lakes moderate otherwise harsh winters.
content.soil: Long Island: well-drained glacial soils. Finger Lakes: varied glacial deposits.
key_facts: Finger Lakes renowned for dry Riesling

content.overview:
Since 1650, and more than 100 years before the Mission grape reached California, its juice was being fermented for the needs of Spanish friars in Arizona, New Mexico, and near El Paso in Texas. Texas has a special place in the history of the vine. It is the botanical heart of America — and can boast more indigenous grapevine species than any other region on earth. Of 36 species of the genus Vitis, no fewer than 15 are Texas natives. Thomas V Munson of Denison, Texas, made hundreds of hybrids in his search for immune rootstock. It was a Texan who saved not only France's but the whole world's wine industry.

In the early 1970s a new start was made with experimental plantings of vinifera and hybrid vines in the High Plains region near Lubbock. Despite the infinite exposure and dismal flatness, its soil is deep, calcareous, and fertile, its sunshine brilliant, its nights cool. Texas now boasts more than 200 wineries.

It is the Rockies that allow New Mexico to even think of growing wine: elevation cools the climate down. New Mexico has a national reputation for the fine sparkling wine made by the Gruet winery.

Southeast Arizona, with the Sonoita AVA, and Colorado's Grand Valley AVA are also emerging wine regions.
content.climate: Texas High Plains: brilliant sunshine, cool nights, very cold winters. New Mexico: high altitude (4,500-7,000ft) moderates temperatures. Colorado: continental at 4,000ft elevation.
content.soil: Texas: deep, calcareous, fertile in High Plains. Arizona: varied in Sonoita. Colorado: varied in Grand Valley.
key_facts: Texas is botanical heart of America with 15 native Vitis species
Thomas V Munson saved world wine industry with phylloxera-resistant rootstocks
Gruet sparkling wine is New Mexico's claim to fame
High altitude is key to New Mexico and Colorado viticulture

canada.name: Canada
canada.overview:
Canada may, on the face of it, seem a decidedly chilly place to grow vines, and only in the past 20 years or so has any wine made in Canada merited export, but good wine is now made with increasing elan in four different provinces. The modern industry dates only from the signing of the Free Trade Agreement with the USA in the late 1980s, which encouraged the revolutionary replacement of American and hybrid grape varieties with European Vitis vinifera vines.

Ontario was long the dominant wine province, but British Columbia is catching up fast. Wines made in the brilliant blue light and almost desert conditions of the Okanagan Valley are distinguished by the same sort of brightness of fruit as is found in Washington State to the south.

On Canada's east coast, Nova Scotia has 16 wineries growing mainly winter-hardy hybrids. Quebec also has a small wine industry dependent on winter-hardy hybrids, many bred at the University of Minnesota.

content.overview:
British Columbia now has nearly 10,000 acres of mostly vinifera vines, more than 200 wineries, and 900 mostly small vineyards. The Okanagan Valley lies in a rain shadow 200 miles (320km) east of Vancouver, where a series of lakes, the largest being the long, narrow, deep Lake Okanagan, ward off winter freeze. Irrigation is essential in the southern Okanagan.

Summer days may be torrid but nights are almost always cool. Autumn arrives early so early-ripening grapes are generally favoured and this may be one of Pinot Blanc's perfect spots. Riesling and Chardonnay tend to shine although there are some fine reds, including Bordeaux blends, and not just in the far south of Okanagan, where Lake Osoyoos repeats the long format of Lake Okanagan.

Similkameen Valley to the southwest is British Columbia's second most important wine region and favours red wines. Fraser Valley just outside Vancouver is usefully frost-free. On Vancouver Island and the Gulf Islands, the climate may be much cooler and wetter than Okanagan's but winemaking enthusiasm is by no means dampened.
content.climate: Semi-desert conditions in inland Okanagan. Rain shadow with irrigation essential. Summer days torrid, nights almost always cool. Autumn arrives early.
content.soil: Varied, including sandy and volcanic soils. Semi-desert conditions similar to eastern Washington.
key_facts: Nearly 10,000 acres of mostly vinifera vines
Rain shadow 200 miles east of Vancouver
Lake Okanagan wards off winter freeze
Pinot Blanc may be perfect for this climate

content.overview:
The province of Ontario produces more than three out of every four bottles of Canadian wine, from more than 200 wineries in four Designated Viticultural Areas (DVAs). It was the discovery of the potential for Icewine, pressed from grapes frozen solid, their sugar concentrated to formidable intensity, that kick-started the industry in the 1970s. Most of Ontario's wine is sold in the province, although the prodigious quantities of Icewine produced each year are a lucrative export commodity, especially to Japan.

The Niagara Peninsula is Canada's most important wine region, with 13,500 acres of Ontario's total 16,000 acres of vineyard. A combination of geographical quirks has long made viticulture possible in the semi-continental climate here. This narrow mosaic of glacial deposits is protected by the Great Lakes both north and south and the deep Niagara River to the east. These vast bodies of water delay budbreak in spring and prolong ripening in autumn.

Riesling is arguably Niagara's strength for bone-dry wines, too, although individual producers have shown occasional brilliance with Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, Gamay, and even Syrah. The most recent DVA, Prince Edward County on Lake Ontario's north shore, is much cooler, but its shallow limestone soils are already showing potential for fine Chardonnay and Pinot Noir.
content.climate: Average growing season temperature 60.1°F (15.6°C) at St Catharines. Annual rainfall 29in (746mm). Semi-continental, moderated by Great Lakes. Principal hazards: winter freeze, underripeness.
content.soil: Glacial deposits. Niagara benches with calcareous soils particularly suitable for Riesling and Pinot Noir. Prince Edward County: shallow limestone.
key_facts: Niagara divided into 12 regional appellations and sub-appellations
Prince Edward County shows potential for Pinot Noir

content.overview:
Mexico has the oldest wine industry of the New World, founded in the 1530s when Governor Hernando Cortes decreed that all farmers plant 10 grapevines a year for every Indian slave on their estate, but it joined the modern wine era only very recently. In 1699, the king of Spain banned new vineyards in Mexico to protect the Spanish wine industry, thus setting back the development of a wine culture in Mexico for three centuries.

Barely 10% of Mexico's 100,000 acres of vines are dedicated to table wine; the great majority produces table grapes, raisins, and, especially, brandy. Today Baja California is responsible for a good 90% of all Mexican wine, produced from about 40 wineries.

The Guadalupe Valley, just 60 miles south of the troubled border town of Tijuana, is the home of many of Mexico's new wave of ambitious vintners. Water is in very short supply so the wines from well-established vines, rarely troubled by pests and diseases, are remarkably intense. The vineyards are regularly cooled at night by Pacific fog and breezes blowing through Baja's east-west valleys.

An important new direction was taken in 2005, when a consortium led by renowned winemaker Hugo D'Acosta acquired more than 450 acres. Called Paralelo, the super-ecological operation's aim is to spread the wine gospel locally and to make quintessentially Mexican wines rather than copies of those made over the border in California.
content.climate: Mediterranean influenced by Pacific fog and breezes. Water in very short supply. Nights cooled by ocean influence through east-west valleys.
content.soil: Sandy soils on valley floors help keep phylloxera at bay. Vines planted at 650-1,650ft (200-500m) elevation.
key_facts: Oldest wine industry in New World (1530s)
Baja California produces 90% of Mexican wine
Guadalupe Valley is center of quality wine production
Water scarcity produces remarkably intense wines
Hugo D'Acosta's Paralelo leading ecological winemaking

content.overview: Climate change has played straight into the hands of those growing vines in the British Isles. England's wine-growers now have such confidence that nearly 4,000 acres (1,600ha) of vineyard are scattered widely over the southern half of the country, with the greatest concentration in the southeast — the counties of Kent, East and West Sussex, and Surrey. A large number of smaller vineyards (there are more than 500 in total) are also found across the south to the West Country, along the Thames and Severn valleys, and in East Anglia, the driest part of England, as well as in southern Wales and Ireland. The largest grower is sparkling pioneer Nyetimber, with 410 acres (166ha) of vines in West Sussex and Hampshire. About 80% of English wine is white, with most of the rest rosé. The Champagne varieties Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Meunier already account for 45% of the total area, a proportion expected to rise to 75% as new vineyards are planted and older ones pulled out. Bacchus, Seyval Blanc, Reichensteiner, Müller-Thurgau, Madeleine x Angevine 7672, the red-fleshed hybrid Rondo, Schönburger, Ortega, and Pinot Blanc are the next most widely grown varieties.
content.climate: Average growing season temperature 57.3°F (14.1°C). Average annual rainfall 26in (648mm). Harvest month rainfall October: 2.9in (74mm). Principal viticultural hazards: Poor fruit set, high acids in cooler years, low yields.
content.soil: Chalk soils similar to Champagne in the Downs region
key_facts: Average production over 2.5 million bottles per year

content.overview: Even now, in a more open and curious wine world than ever before, Swiss wine remains little-known beyond its national borders. Less than 1% is exported, and tourists tend to stick to a few staples that are far from being the best. Hardly anyone knows that over 200 different wine grapes are grown in this small country. The wine market was opened up in 2001, and from 2006 the once-routine stretching of Swiss with imported wine was banned. The Swiss are enthusiastic wine drinkers and import about 60% of their needs — including a great deal of Burgundy's best. Making any sort of wine in a country with Switzerland's prices is inevitably expensive. The country's total of 36,922 acres (14,942ha) of vineyards are divided between thousands of full- and part-time growers, whose impeccable and often spectacular plots are attentively gardened, rather than commercially farmed.
content.climate: Switzerland has some of the highest vineyards in Europe and is home to the first vineyards on two of the world's great wine rivers, the Rhine and the Rhône, which both rise in the Gotthard Massif.
key_facts: Chasselas is the most-planted white grape

content.overview: The steep sides of the Valais, the valley which the young River Rhône carved through the Alps, are followed by gentler slopes in Vaud. In the high Valais, peculiarly alpine conditions — brilliant sun and summer drought — can make concentrated, super-ripe wines. The average rainfall in Sion, a major wine centre, is less than two-thirds that of Bordeaux; Valais growers have built bisses, steep channels of mountain water, to irrigate their vines since the Middle Ages. The Valais' 12,530 acres (5,070ha) of vineyards are tended by as many as 20,000 growers, only about 500 of whom make wine. About a fifth of Valais grapes are processed by the dominant co-operative, Provins.
content.climate: Average growing season temperature 58.7°F (14.9°C). Average annual rainfall 24in (599mm). Principal viticultural hazards: Spring frost.
content.soil: Varied alpine soils
key_facts: Some of Europe's highest vines at Visperterminen, at 3,600ft (1,100m)

content.overview: The vineyards in Vaud are quite different from Valais, not concentrated by alpine sunshine but gently ripened by the mild climate round Lake Geneva (Lac Léman). Vaud is the traditional heart of Swiss wine, where Cistercian monks introduced viticulture from Burgundy more than 900 years ago. Although red wine is gaining ground, 61% of vineyards are devoted to a single white grape, Chasselas — never specified on Vaud labels, which favour geographical names. Yields are generally relatively high, but the best lakeside vineyards manage to produce the world's most characterful expressions of this mild grape.
content.climate: Mild climate moderated by Lake Geneva
content.soil: Clay and limestone in Lavaux; varied in other areas
key_facts: Lavaux terraces UNESCO World Heritage Site (2007)
Two Grands Crus: Calamin and Dézaley
Cistercian monks introduced viticulture 900+ years ago

content.overview: Geneva, at the end of Lake Geneva, is hard at work upgrading its wines. Geneva's vineyards have changed more than any in Switzerland in recent years. Gamay is now the principal grape, having overtaken Chasselas, and is followed by Pinot Noir, Gamaret, and Chardonnay. There are three main vineyard areas, the largest being Mandement (Satigny is Switzerland's biggest wine commune), which has the ripest and tastiest Chasselas.
key_facts: Satigny is Switzerland's biggest wine commune
Three main vineyard areas including Mandement

content.overview: Austria's array of intensely pure wines have their own distinct, finely etched personality. There is something of the freshness of the Rhine in them, more perhaps of the fieriness and high flavour of the Danube (Donau in German), but almost nothing in common with Austria's wines of 30 years ago, before the country underwent vinous revolution — all of it benign. So-called Weinland Österreich in the far east of the country around Vienna, where the Alps descend to the great Pannonian Plain that reaches across Hungary, is where most Austrian wine is made, in hugely varied conditions. There is slate, sand, clay, gneiss, loam, and fertile loess, parched fields and perpetually green ones, craggy precipices above the Danube, and the tranquil shallow mere of the Neusiedler See.
content.climate: Latitude/Altitude: 48.42° / 679ft (207m) at Krems. Average growing season temperature 58.5°F (14.7°C). Average annual rainfall 20in (516mm). Principal viticultural hazards: Spring frost.
content.soil: Slate, sand, clay, gneiss, loam, and fertile loess
key_facts: Grüner Veltliner grown on more than a third of vineyard

content.overview: If ever a region needed an atlas to tell its story, it is the Wachau, a complex meeting point of northern and southern climates and a rich mosaic of different soils and rocks. Forty miles (65km) before it reaches Vienna, the broad grey Danube broaches a range of 1,600ft (490m) high hills. For a short stretch the craggy north bank of the river, as steep as the Mosel or Côte-Rôtie, is patchworked with vines on ledges and outcrops along narrow paths leading up from the river to the crowning woods. This is the Wachau, Austria's most famous wine region, even if, with only 3,335 acres (1,350ha), it constitutes just 3% of the country's vineyard.
content.climate: Hot Pannonian summers cooled by northern night air. Rainfall often falls below 20in/500mm a year.
content.soil: Gneiss and granite on upper slopes, loess and sand on lower banks
key_facts: Codex Wachau classification: Steinfeder, Federspiel, Smaragd

content.overview: The flat and often sandy shores of Lake Neusiedl, an extraordinary giant marshy pool more than 20 miles (32km) long and only 3ft (1m) deep, are today the slightly improbable source of Austria's greatest red and sweet white wines. For a long time, Burgenland seemed as though it was from an earlier era of Middle Europe, the days of the Hapsburgs and the Esterházys, when Austria and Hungary were the same empire. Now it grows about 34,600 acres (14,000ha) of carefully tended vines — 4,940 acres (2,000ha) of them in Seewinkel.
content.climate: Austria's hottest wine region, wide open to Pannonian warmth
content.soil: Sandy shores around Lake Neusiedl; loess in Mittelburgenland
key_facts: Austria's greatest red and sweet white wines
Four DACs: Eisenberg, Mittelburgenland, Leithaberg, Neusiedlersee

content.soil: Gneiss and granite on upper slopes, loess and sand on lower banks
key_facts: Codex Wachau classification: Steinfeder, Federspiel, Smaragd

content.overview: If the Wachau made the running in Austria's initial assault on the world's lovers of dry white wines, it did not take long for them to notice that the neighbouring Kremstal and Kamptal made wines of similar quality and style — and in many cases charged less for them. The pretty twin towns of Stein and Krems mark the eastern end of the Wachau and the start of the very similar but slightly less dramatic Kremstal. The clay and limestone vineyards around here, including the famous Steiner Hund, can give particular density to both Riesling and Grüner Veltliner.
content.climate: About 1.8°F (1°C) warmer than Wachau
content.soil: Clay and limestone in Kremstal; loess in Kamptal
key_facts: Main river influence from south-flowing Kamp tributary

content.overview: The flat and often sandy shores of Lake Neusiedl, an extraordinary giant marshy pool more than 20 miles (32km) long and only 3ft (1m) deep, are today the slightly improbable source of Austria's greatest red and sweet white wines. content.climate: Austria's hottest wine region, wide open to Pannonian warmth
content.soil: Sandy shores around Lake Neusiedl; loess in Mittelburgenland
key_facts: Austria's greatest red and sweet white wines
Four DACs: Eisenberg, Mittelburgenland, Leithaberg, Neusiedlersee

content.overview: Steiermark (Styria), away to the south, has little in common with Austria's northerly regions. For decades it has produced exclusively dry wines, not unlike those over the border in eastern Slovenia where some Austrian producers now operate. It may have only 7% of the country's vineyards, and those widely dispersed, but its reputation for intense, piercing Sauvignon Blanc (sometimes oaked), Chardonnay, and Welschriesling is unmatched within Austria. Chardonnay, some of it travelling most unusually under a local alias, Morillon, is well entrenched here.
content.climate: Cooler than northern regions, influenced by border with Slovenia
content.soil: High schists in Sausal; volcanic soils in Klöch

content.overview: For centuries, Hungary has had the most distinctive food and wine culture, the most varied native grapes, and the most refined wine laws and customs of any country east of Germany — and these are once again being cherished. The country's extraordinary palette of indigenous white grape varieties, with Furmint currently to the fore, is at last recognized as an asset rather than a liability. Today, Hungarian wine producers confidently offer definitively well-made, post-communist wines in a wide range of recognizably Magyar/Hungarian styles.
content.climate: Average annual mean temperatures are warmest in the south, reaching 52.5°F (11.4°C) around Pécs, and coolest in the north, reaching a low of around 49°F (9.5°C) at Sopron.
content.soil: Varied: volcanic in Tokaj, loess in Szekszárd, sandy in Great Plain
key_facts: Extraordinary palette of indigenous grape varieties
Furmint currently the leading white variety
Half of vineyard area on Great Plain

content.overview: The word 'legend' is more often used about Tokaji than any other wine. (Tokay is the old English spelling; the town that inspired the name, at the bottom of the map, Tokaj). And with good reason. Although it suffered a temporary total eclipse of standards during the communist era, Tokaji has been legendary for 400 years. Only champagne has spawned as many anecdotes. Tokaji was the first wine knowingly to be made from botrytised or 'nobly rotten' grapes: over a century before Rhine wine, and perhaps two before Sauternes.
content.climate: Average growing season temperature 60.4°F (15.8°C). Average annual rainfall 24in (620mm). Principal viticultural hazards: Autumn rain, grey rot.
content.soil: Volcanic soils from Zemplén Mountains
key_facts: First wine knowingly made from botrytised grapes

content.soil: Volcanic soils from Zemplén Mountains
key_facts: First wine knowingly made from botrytised grapes

content.overview: A wine prospector in these cultured but battered countries 20 years ago would have found very little. But the Czechs and Slovaks are becoming wine drinkers again. Quality has risen with quantity. Little is exported yet, but the potential is there.
content.climate: Continental, similar to neighboring Austria's Weinviertel
content.soil: Limestone in Pálava hills; varied elsewhere

content.overview: Wine was traditionally more important in Slovakia than the Czech Republic, land of Pilsner, but the total area of Slovak vineyard halved to 37,000 acres (15,000ha) between 1980 and 2010. Progress — bringing, for example, malolactic fermentation, oak ageing and lees contact — is changing the picture. Sweet Icewine and straw wine, made from dried grapes, have been revived. White wines predominate, with international grapes (Riesling, Chardonnay, Sauvignon Blanc, as well as Cabernet Sauvignon and various Pinots) in the forefront.
content.climate: Warmer in south, cooler in Malé Karpaty hills
content.soil: Fertile soils in south; stonier in Malé Karpaty hills

content.overview: Istria and Dalmatia have always been the frontispiece of Croatia (Hrvatska), the sublime coast of golden Venetian ports and hundreds of islands. Emperors, crusaders, and doges passed up and down without, perhaps, doing much wine tasting. Today's yacht-owners know better. Croatia's wines are original, well-made, and fetching good prices. Croatia is split into two very different regions by the Dinaric Alps mountain range that follows the coast. Coastal Croatia consists of Croatian Istria, the Croatian Coast, Northern Dalmatia, Central and Southern Dalmatia and associated islands, and the Dalmatian Highlands. It is a white wine coast in the main.
content.climate: Mediterranean on the coast, continental inland
content.soil: Varied: limestone on coast, clay and sand inland
key_facts: Over 1,000 islands, many with unique grape varieties
Graševina (Welschriesling) covers a quarter of vineyards
Tribidrag (Crljenak Kaštelanski) is identical to Zinfandel

content.overview: Istria, the most northerly of the coastal regions, is blessed with a better grape: its very own Malvasia, Malvazija Istarska. It reliably makes firm whites redolent of honey and apple skins, especially when its makers follow the Slovenian fashion of macerating the grape skins in their juice. Acacia rather than oak is often the barrel-wood of choice in Istria — despite the fact that Slavonia, not far to the east, is famous for the quality of its oak. The result is often lively, full-bodied, complex wines — some even fit for ageing.
content.climate: Mediterranean with Adriatic influence
content.soil: Terra rossa (red soil) and limestone
key_facts: Malvazija Istarska is the signature white grape
Teran (Refosco) is the distinctive red variety
Acacia barrels often preferred over oak

content.overview: There is more excitement going south down the coast, where the Malvasia may not have the same qualities, but is varied by local grapes of characters only now being exploited. Red Plavac Mali is the workhorse grape of the coast, usually savoury, dense and powerful in Dingač and pungent Postup from the steep seaside terraces of the Pelješac Peninsula north of Dubrovnik.
content.climate: Mediterranean with hot, dry summers
content.soil: Limestone and rocky soils on steep terraces
key_facts: Plavac Mali is the workhorse red grape
Dingač and Postup are famous appellations on Pelješac Peninsula

content.soil: Limestone and rocky soils on steep terraces
